main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Dual Random Networks Distillation (DuRND) framework","Use of two lightweight random network modules (success RN and failure RN)","Joint computation of novelty reward and contribution reward","Dynamic reward adjustment scheme evolving from novelty to contribution"],"problems":["Balancing exploration and exploitation in reinforcement learning, especially in sparse-reward tasks","Inefficiency of existing reward shaping methods that focus only on novelty or value-based rewards","High computational overhead of some reward shaping methods in high-dimensional environments"],"datasets":["Atari","VizDoom","MiniWorld"],"metrics":["Performance compared to benchmarks (e.g., cumulative reward, learning efficiency, convergence stability)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes DuRND, a novel framework that integrates two random network modules to jointly compute novelty and contribution rewards for reinforcement learning.","DuRND dynamically evolves the reward structure from exploration (novelty) to exploitation (contribution) as learning progresses, achieving efficient exploration-exploitation balance.","DuRND operates with low computational overhead, requiring only two random network modules, and does not rely on auxiliary agents, historical state buffers, or pseudocount estimation.","Demonstrates superior performance and scalability in high-dimensional, sparse-reward environments compared to several benchmarks."]}

methods: Dual Random Networks Distillation (DuRND) framework
Use of two lightweight random network modules (success RN and failure RN)
Joint computation of novelty reward and contribution reward
Dynamic reward adjustment scheme evolving from novelty to contribution
problems: Balancing exploration and exploitation in reinforcement learning, especially in sparse-reward tasks
Inefficiency of existing reward shaping methods that focus only on novelty or value-based rewards
High computational overhead of some reward shaping methods in high-dimensional environments
metrics: Performance compared to benchmarks (e.g., cumulative reward, learning efficiency, convergence stability)
novelty_claims: Proposes DuRND, a novel framework that integrates two random network modules to jointly compute novelty and contribution rewards for reinforcement learning.
DuRND dynamically evolves the reward structure from exploration (novelty) to exploitation (contribution) as learning progresses, achieving efficient exploration-exploitation balance.
DuRND operates with low computational overhead, requiring only two random network modules, and does not rely on auxiliary agents, historical state buffers, or pseudocount estimation.
Demonstrates superior performance and scalability in high-dimensional, sparse-reward environments compared to several benchmarks.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Distributional Random Network Distillation (DRND)","DRND combined with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) for online RL","SAC-DRND: DRND as an anti-exploration penalty in Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) for offline RL"],"problems":["Bonus inconsistency in Random Network Distillation (RND) for exploration in deep reinforcement learning","Improving discriminative power and precision of exploration bonus allocation","Effective exploration in sparse or complex reward environments","Providing anti-exploration mechanisms in offline RL settings"],"datasets":["Montezuma’s Revenge (Atari)","Gravitar (Atari)","Venture (Atari)","Continuous-control gym-robotics environments","D4RL locomotion datasets","D4RL Antmaze datasets"],"metrics":["Return (score)","Exploration bonus precision","State transition frequency estimation accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Return on Montezuma’s Revenge, Gravitar, Venture (Atari)","value":"DRND outperforms PPO, RND, CFN, and ICM baselines"},{"metric":"Return on continuous-control gym-robotics environments","value":"DRND outperforms existing approaches"},{"metric":"Return on D4RL locomotion and Antmaze datasets (offline RL)","value":"SAC-DRND outperforms many recent strong baselines"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Distributional RND (DRND), which distills a distribution of random networks for improved exploration bonus allocation","DRND implicitly incorporates pseudo-counts, merging count-based and RND advantages without extra computational or spatial overhead","DRND provides improved resilience to initial state value variations, more accurate state transition frequency estimation, and better discrimination of dataset distributions compared to RND","DRND can serve as both an exploration and anti-exploration mechanism, effective in both online and offline RL settings","SAC-DRND is a novel offline RL algorithm leveraging DRND as an anti-exploration penalty"]}

methods: Distributional Random Network Distillation (DRND)
DRND combined with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) for online RL
SAC-DRND: DRND as an anti-exploration penalty in Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) for offline RL
problems: Bonus inconsistency in Random Network Distillation (RND) for exploration in deep reinforcement learning
Improving discriminative power and precision of exploration bonus allocation
Effective exploration in sparse or complex reward environments
Providing anti-exploration mechanisms in offline RL settings

results.metric: Return on Montezuma’s Revenge, Gravitar, Venture (Atari)
results.value: DRND outperforms PPO, RND, CFN, and ICM baselines
DRND outperforms existing approaches

results.metric: Return on D4RL locomotion and Antmaze datasets (offline RL)
results.value: SAC-DRND outperforms many recent strong baselines

novelty_claims: Introduction of Distributional RND (DRND), which distills a distribution of random networks for improved exploration bonus allocation
DRND implicitly incorporates pseudo-counts, merging count-based and RND advantages without extra computational or spatial overhead
DRND provides improved resilience to initial state value variations, more accurate state transition frequency estimation, and better discrimination of dataset distributions compared to RND
DRND can serve as both an exploration and anti-exploration mechanism, effective in both online and offline RL settings
SAC-DRND is a novel offline RL algorithm leveraging DRND as an anti-exploration penalty

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SERENE (SparsE Reward Exploration via Novelty and Emitters) algorithm","Alternating Novelty Search for exploration and emitters for exploitation","Meta-scheduler for computational budget allocation between exploration and exploitation"],"problems":["Efficient exploration in sparse reward environments","Balancing exploration and exploitation in reinforcement learning with sparse and disjoint reward areas","Overcoming limitations of standard RL and existing QD algorithms in sparse reward settings"],"datasets":["Various sparse reward environments (specific environments not named in provided content)"],"metrics":["Performance compared to baselines (likely reward achieved, diversity of solutions, but not explicitly named in provided content)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance compared to baselines","value":"SERENE compares favorably to existing baselines in sparse reward environments"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of SERENE, a QD algorithm that alternates between pure exploration (Novelty Search) and exploitation (emitters) via a meta-scheduler","Clear separation of exploration and exploitation processes, unlike previous approaches that merge them","Ability to discover and efficiently exploit multiple disjoint reward areas in sparse reward settings","Returns both a diverse set of solutions and high-performing solutions for each reward area"]}

methods: SERENE (SparsE Reward Exploration via Novelty and Emitters) algorithm
Alternating Novelty Search for exploration and emitters for exploitation
Meta-scheduler for computational budget allocation between exploration and exploitation
problems: Efficient exploration in sparse reward environments
Balancing exploration and exploitation in reinforcement learning with sparse and disjoint reward areas
Overcoming limitations of standard RL and existing QD algorithms in sparse reward settings
datasets: Various sparse reward environments (specific environments not named in provided content)
metrics: Performance compared to baselines (likely reward achieved, diversity of solutions, but not explicitly named in provided content)

results.value: SERENE compares favorably to existing baselines in sparse reward environments

novelty_claims: Introduction of SERENE, a QD algorithm that alternates between pure exploration (Novelty Search) and exploitation (emitters) via a meta-scheduler
Clear separation of exploration and exploitation processes, unlike previous approaches that merge them
Ability to discover and efficiently exploit multiple disjoint reward areas in sparse reward settings
Returns both a diverse set of solutions and high-performing solutions for each reward area

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Sibling Rivalry: a model-free, dynamic reward shaping method using sibling trajectories (pairs of rollouts with the same policy, starting state, and goal) to balance exploration and exploitation and avoid local optima in distance-to-goal reward shaping"],"problems":["Difficulty of solving sparse reward tasks in reinforcement learning due to poor exploration and local optima induced by naive distance-to-goal reward shaping","Need for reward shaping methods that do not require extensive domain expertise or problem-specific engineering"],"datasets":["Continuous navigation tasks","Discrete bit flipping tasks","3D navigation tasks","3D construction tasks in a Minecraft environment"],"metrics":["Task success rate (solving the goal)","Learning efficiency (speed of convergence)"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of Sibling Rivalry, a self-balancing shaped reward method that uses sibling rollouts to dynamically adjust reward shaping and avoid local optima without additional reward engineering or domain expertise","The method converges to the original sparse reward objective as the agent learns, preserving optimality","Demonstrated effectiveness on a variety of hard-exploration tasks where naive distance-based shaping and other exploration strategies fail"]}

methods: Sibling Rivalry: a model-free, dynamic reward shaping method using sibling trajectories (pairs of rollouts with the same policy, starting state, and goal) to balance exploration and exploitation and avoid local optima in distance-to-goal reward shaping
problems: Difficulty of solving sparse reward tasks in reinforcement learning due to poor exploration and local optima induced by naive distance-to-goal reward shaping
Need for reward shaping methods that do not require extensive domain expertise or problem-specific engineering
datasets: 3D construction tasks in a Minecraft environment
novelty_claims: Introduction of Sibling Rivalry, a self-balancing shaped reward method that uses sibling rollouts to dynamically adjust reward shaping and avoid local optima without additional reward engineering or domain expertise
The method converges to the original sparse reward objective as the agent learns, preserving optimality
Demonstrated effectiveness on a variety of hard-exploration tasks where naive distance-based shaping and other exploration strategies fail

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Constrained joint policy diversity for exploration","Joint policy diversity measurement (unbalanced diversity)","Filtering-based exploration constraint","Enhancement of classical count-based exploration with policy-level diversity"],"problems":["Inefficient exploration in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) under sparse-reward settings","Vulnerability of classical count-based exploration methods when combined with neural network parameterized agents in MARL","Insufficiency of state-level exploration bonuses for efficient exploration in MARL"],"datasets":["Multiple-Particle Environment (MPE) (discrete version)","Google Research Football (GRF)","StarCraft II micromanagement (SMAC), including the 3s_vs_5z task"],"metrics":["Sample efficiency (normalized sample size to achieve >50% success rate)","Success rate","Number of explored states (in reward-free setting)"],"results":[{"metric":"Sample efficiency (normalized sample size to >50% success rate, SMAC)","value":"Our method achieves better or comparable sample efficiency than baselines; some baselines fail to reach 50% success rate (infinite sample size)"},{"metric":"Success rate (3s_vs_5z, SMAC, sparse-reward)","value":"Our method is the first to learn winning strategies without domain knowledge"},{"metric":"Number of explored states (reward-free setting)","value":"Our method explores significantly more states than classical count-based methods"}],"novelty_claims":["First to introduce constrained joint policy diversity to enhance classical count-based exploration in MARL with neural network agents under sparse rewards","Propose a filtering-based exploration constraint to refine joint policy diversity for effective exploration","First to achieve winning strategies on hard SMAC tasks (e.g., 3s_vs_5z) without domain knowledge under sparse-reward settings","Demonstrate that policy-level diversity can significantly improve exploration over state-level bonuses in MARL"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Rewarding Impact-Driven Exploration (RIDE): a novel intrinsic reward method that encourages agents to take actions leading to significant changes in their learned state representation, using both a forward and an inverse dynamics model."],"problems":["Exploration in sparse reward environments for model-free reinforcement learning.","Ineffectiveness of existing intrinsic reward methods in procedurally-generated environments where states are rarely revisited.","Diminishing intrinsic rewards during training in prior methods.","Generalization of RL agents to new, unseen environments in procedurally-generated settings."],"datasets":["MiniGrid (procedurally-generated grid-world environments)","Singleton environments with high-dimensional observations (visual inputs) used in prior work"],"metrics":["Sample efficiency","Cumulative reward","Qualitative analysis of learned behavior and intrinsic reward dynamics"],"results":[{"metric":"Sample efficiency (procedurally-generated MiniGrid)","value":"RIDE is more sample efficient than existing exploration methods (quantitative values not specified in provided text)"},{"metric":"Intrinsic reward dynamics","value":"RIDE's intrinsic reward does not diminish during training, unlike prior methods (qualitative result)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel intrinsic reward (RIDE) that encourages impactful changes in the agent's learned state representation, rather than just visiting novel states.","RIDE uses both a forward and inverse dynamics model to learn the state representation for intrinsic reward calculation.","Demonstrates that RIDE is more effective than prior intrinsic reward methods in procedurally-generated environments, where state revisitation is rare.","Shows that RIDE's intrinsic reward does not diminish during training, unlike previous approaches, and better incentivizes interaction with controllable objects."]}

methods: Rewarding Impact-Driven Exploration (RIDE): a novel intrinsic reward method that encourages agents to take actions leading to significant changes in their learned state representation, using both a forward and an inverse dynamics model.
problems: Exploration in sparse reward environments for model-free reinforcement learning.
Ineffectiveness of existing intrinsic reward methods in procedurally-generated environments where states are rarely revisited.
Diminishing intrinsic rewards during training in prior methods.
Generalization of RL agents to new, unseen environments in procedurally-generated settings.
datasets: Singleton environments with high-dimensional observations (visual inputs) used in prior work
metrics: Qualitative analysis of learned behavior and intrinsic reward dynamics

results.value: RIDE is more sample efficient than existing exploration methods (quantitative values not specified in provided text)
RIDE's intrinsic reward does not diminish during training, unlike prior methods (qualitative result)

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel intrinsic reward (RIDE) that encourages impactful changes in the agent's learned state representation, rather than just visiting novel states.
RIDE uses both a forward and inverse dynamics model to learn the state representation for intrinsic reward calculation.
Demonstrates that RIDE is more effective than prior intrinsic reward methods in procedurally-generated environments, where state revisitation is rare.
Shows that RIDE's intrinsic reward does not diminish during training, unlike previous approaches, and better incentivizes interaction with controllable objects.

novelty_claims: Introduction of the k-Shortest-Path (k-SP) constraint for RL, which prunes suboptimal trajectories and improves sample efficiency in sparse-reward tasks.
Proof that the k-SP constraint preserves optimality for any MDP (with mild assumptions).
Development of a soft cost function formulation for the k-SP constraint, applicable to both policy-based and value-based RL methods.
Practical implementation using a reachability network to efficiently enforce the constraint in deep RL domains.
Demonstration that the method outperforms existing novelty-seeking exploration methods, including count-based exploration, even in continuous control tasks.

novelty_claims: Proposes SORS, a novel self-supervised online reward shaping algorithm that can be paired with any RL algorithm and does not require external supervision or domain knowledge.
Introduces a classification-based reward inference method that uses sparse rewards as a self-supervised signal to rank trajectories, unlike prior methods that require manual ranking.
Provides theoretical justification that the shaped reward function preserves the optimal policy under certain conditions.
Demonstrates empirically that SORS converges significantly faster than standard RL baselines in sparse-reward environments.

Introduces a new lightweight graph-based diversity metric and a graph-based proximity metric that are obstacle-aware and computationally efficient.
Designs an automatic trade-off mechanism to balance diversity and proximity, applicable to different environments and training stages.
Upgrades the graph-based environment representation to eliminate interference between obstacles and the robot body, improving over prior G-HGG.
